up.method: reported (cited from ref. 25, used to justify the GEM's glucose-uptake constraint)

growth_records.cite: (GEM methods paper; value cited from ref. 25) et al. High-throughput generation and comparison of genome-scale metabolic models reveal strain-specific metabolic diversity in 439 Lactococcus strains. mSystems (2025). DOI:10.1128/msystems.01517-25. [PMC13098203]
growth_records.snippet: close to the experimentally determined glucose uptake rate for L. lactis IL1403, 18-24 mmol g-1 h-1 (ref 25)
growth_records.notes: Number explicit in this GEM paper's Methods but the measurement itself is attributed to an external cited study (ref. 25), not generated by this paper. Rate given here as midpoint (21) of the stated 18-24 mmol/gDW/h range; original range preserved in units field. No medium/temperature/pH details given for the original measurement in this paper's text.

growth_records.medium: CDMpc (chemically defined medium) with 25 mM glucose

up.method: glucose-limited chemostat; consumption confirmed and reported graphically (Fig 1C simulated-vs-measured exchange fluxes; Fig 4) but no numeric value given in text/tables
arginine uptake flux reported as amino-acid flux in Fig 4C (mmol/gCDW/h); direction (uptake) explicit but no numeric value in text

sec.method: mixed-acid fermentation product at D=0.5/h; reported graphically (Fig 1C) only, no numeric value in text
mixed-acid fermentation product; reported graphically (Fig 1C) only, no numeric value in text
mixed-acid fermentation product; reported graphically (Fig 1C) only, no numeric value in text
mixed-acid fermentation product; reported graphically (Fig 1C) only, no numeric value in text

growth_records.cite: Chen et al. Proteome constraints reveal targets for improving microbial fitness in nutrient-rich environments. Molecular Systems Biology (2021). DOI:10.15252/msb.202010093. [PMC8022198]
growth_records.snippet: Chemostat cultivation ... 300 ml bioreactors with 270 ml working volume ... 30C water baths. The headspace was continuously flushed with 5% CO2 and 95% N2. pH was controlled at 6.5 ... CDMpc with 25 mM glucose was added at a rate of 2.25 ml
growth_records.notes: Wild-type L. lactis MG1363, glucose-limited anaerobic chemostat. Growth rate CALCULATED from chemostat steady-state identity mu = D = 0.5/h (dilution rate stated explicitly: feed 2.25 ml/min into 270 ml volume = 0.5/h). Doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.5 = 1.386 h. Temperature 30C, pH 6.5, anaerobic (5% CO2/95% N2 sparge). Medium = CDMpc; only 25 mM glucose stated in this paper, full recipe not given (referenced), so medium_composition_stated=false. Glucose and arginine are consumed and lactate/formate/acetate/ethanol are secreted (mixed-acid fermentation, which the text states dominates at D=0.5/h since the total-proteome constraint is not yet active), but the paper reports these exchange fluxes only graphically (Fig 1C measured exchange fluxes; Fig 4C amino-acid fluxes in mmol/gCDW/h) with NO numeric values in the text or tables, so rates left null rather than invented.

growth_records.strain: SB261 and SB17 (kefir isolates; Blasche et al.)

up.units: qualitative (13C tracing; no specific rate reported)
up.method: 13C-based proteomics: L. lactis preferentially utilizes the glucose moiety of lactose
Yeast provides essential amino acids to L. lactis; complex bidirectional amino-acid exchange detected

sec.method: L. lactis releases the galactose moiety of lactose, which is consumed by yeast (cross-feeding)
Lactate produced by L. lactis; ~70% fully 13C-labelled on lactose-[13C-glucose], ~40% on lactose-[13C-galactose]
Pyruvate detected/secreted; labelling parallels lactate

mfa.note: Circa 70% of pyruvate and lactate fully 13C-labelled during growth on lactose with the glucose moiety labelled
Only ~40% fully labelled when the galactose moiety is labelled, indicating preferential glucose-moiety catabolism by L. lactis
Alanine had ~20% higher labelling when the galactose part of lactose was labelled

growth_records.cite: Gabrielli et al. Unravelling metabolic cross-feeding in a yeast-bacteria community using 13C-based proteomics. Molecular Systems Biology (2023). DOI:10.15252/msb.202211501. [PMC10090948]
growth_records.method: not reported numerically (growth followed as OD/CFU time-courses, means of 3 replicates)
growth_records.snippet: the community was grown on lactose ... with either glucose or galactose fraction labelled with 13C ... Circa 70% of pyruvate and lactate were found to be labelled in all carbon atoms during growth on lactose-[13C-glucose], but only 40% in t
growth_records.notes: 13C peptide-based MFA / cross-feeding study (yeast + L. lactis). NO quantitative specific rates (mmol/gDW/h) or numeric growth rate reported for L. lactis; only concentration/labelling time-courses. Captured qualitative exchange directions from 13C tracing: L. lactis consumes the glucose moiety of lactose and releases galactose (consumed by yeast) plus lactate/pyruvate; yeast provides amino acids to L. lactis. mfa_fluxes here are isotope-labelling percentages, not exchange fluxes. Record retained for the documented cross-feeding topology; rates left null (not quantified).

growth_records.medium: CDMG agar, co-culture spot 2.5 cm from lactate producer LM

up.method: HPLC; derived

sec.units: mmol/L/h (volumetric, 24-33 h window; confounded by LM-diffused lactate)
sec.method: HPLC; derived

growth_records.cite: Hui Liao et al. Slower-growing species promote interspecific cooperation and coexistence under acid stress through cross-feeding. Nature Communications (2026). DOI:10.1038/s41467-025-67395-z. [PMC12816087]
growth_records.snippet: lactate content ... reaching 68.8 mM at 24 h and a maximum of 98.2 mM at 33 h ... the low amount of residual glucose (22.8 mM) at 30 h and consumption was significantly reduced over the next 6 h to 8.66 mM
growth_records.notes: Glucose window 30->36 h: 22.8 - 8.66 = 14.14 mM over 6 h -> 14.14/6 = 2.36 mmol/L/h. Lactate 24->33 h: 98.2 - 68.8 = 29.4 mM over 9 h -> 29.4/9 = 3.27 mmol/L/h. Lactate here includes LM lactate diffusing into the co-culture spot (stated: '68.8 mM ... inconsistency with ... maximum lactate ... in the producer LM at 27 h was due to the lag in diffusion time'), so the lactate rate is NOT purely A01/B01 production - confounded, low confidence. Two-species co-culture; per-species split not resolvable to rates from text.

growth_records.medium: 100 mM lactate-acidified CDMG + public goods supplement

up.units: mM consumed over time (Fig 5C, no numeric endpoint in text)
up.method: HPLC
HPLC
HPLC

growth_records.cite: Hui Liao et al. Slower-growing species promote interspecific cooperation and coexistence under acid stress through cross-feeding. Nature Communications (2026). DOI:10.1038/s41467-025-67395-z. [PMC12816087]
growth_records.snippet: Changes in the content of public goods over time were detected by HPLC when B01 was monocultured in 100 mM lactate-acidified CDMG liquid medium supplemented with public goods, including 6.0 mM pyruvate ..., 2.7 mM glutamate, and 2.0 mM aspa
growth_records.notes: Supplement concentrations explicit (pyruvate 6.0, glutamate 2.7, aspartate 2.0 mM); these are consumed by B01 over time per Fig 5C but the paper gives only a time-course figure, no numeric uptake rate or endpoint in text -> rates left null. Distinct condition (stress-sensitive B01 rescued by cross-fed public goods).

growth_records.medium: CDMG liquid (with lactate stress; stable-period reconstruction)

sec.units: fraction of glucose (~60% of glucose to glutamate+aspartate+pyruvate combined)
sec.method: HPLC
HPLC
HPLC

growth_records.cite: Hui Liao et al. Slower-growing species promote interspecific cooperation and coexistence under acid stress through cross-feeding. Nature Communications (2026). DOI:10.1038/s41467-025-67395-z. [PMC12816087]
growth_records.snippet: A01 continues to metabolize nearly 60% of glucose into glutamate, aspartate, and pyruvate, secreting them, possibly providing growth for B01 in the stable period
growth_records.notes: Only a fraction is given: ~60% of glucose secreted as combined glutamate+aspartate+pyruvate during the acid-stress stable period; no absolute concentrations, no per-compound split, no timing in text -> no rate derivable. Pure-culture accumulations were noted as 'small' (Figs S20B-D). Included to document A01's stress-induced public-goods secretion phenotype.

growth_records.medium: Chemically defined medium, lactose-limited, with citrate (Condition 1)

up.method: HPLC (Aminex HPX-87H); biomass-specific rate reported graphically in Fig 1
enzymatic (Roche citric acid kit) in chemostat + HPLC in resting-cell assay; approx max ~65 umol/min/gDW at highest citP expression (low pH + citrate)

sec.method: HPLC; ~68 mol% of end-products at pH 5.5 lactose-limited (rest = mixture of formate, acetate, ethanol)
HPLC; part of mixed-acid products (Fig 6)
HPLC; part of mixed-acid products (Fig 6)
HPLC; part of mixed-acid products (Fig 6)
HPLC; mainly produced at low pH in presence of citrate; acetoin yield on citrate highest at pH 5.5 (Fig 7)
HPLC; significant pyruvate efflux at low pH (Fig 6)

growth_records.cite: van Mastrigt, Oscar et al. Citrate, low pH and amino acid limitation induce citrate utilization in Lactococcus lactis biovar diacetylactis. Microbial Biotechnology (2018). DOI:10.1111/1751-7915.13031. [PMC5812246]
growth_records.method: reported (D set = mu at steady state, 0.135 +/- 0.004 /h); doubling time calculated td=ln2/mu=0.6931/0.135=5.13 h
growth_records.snippet: L. lactis biovar diacetylactis FM03-V1 was grown in independent duplicate chemostat cultivations at a growth rate of 0.135 +/- 0.004 h-1 ... more than 99.7% was consumed at pH 5.5 ... Citrate utilization rates increased from approximately 1
growth_records.notes: Chemostat: D=mu=0.135/h reported; td=ln2/0.135=5.13 h. Citrate rate 3.9 mmol/gDW/h = 65 umol/min/gDW x 60/1000 (paper-stated maximum at low pH + citrate, this condition). Per-condition lactose/product rates are reported only graphically (Fig 1, Fig 6) so numeric values left null. Lactate = 68 mol% of end-products for lactose-limited pH 5.5. Citrate consumption >99.7% at pH 5.5. Medium composition fully stated in Methods.

growth_records.medium: Chemically defined medium, lactose-limited, without citrate (Condition 2)

up.method: HPLC; biomass-specific rate reported graphically (Fig 1)

sec.method: HPLC (Fig 6); mixed-acid/homolactic at pH 5.5
HPLC (Fig 6)
HPLC (Fig 6)
HPLC (Fig 6)

growth_records.cite: van Mastrigt, Oscar et al. Citrate, low pH and amino acid limitation induce citrate utilization in Lactococcus lactis biovar diacetylactis. Microbial Biotechnology (2018). DOI:10.1111/1751-7915.13031. [PMC5812246]
growth_records.method: reported (D=mu=0.135/h at steady state); td calculated ln2/0.135=5.13 h
growth_records.snippet: Under lactose limitation, only 68% and 29% of the end-products were lactate at pH 5.5 and 7 respectively. Instead, a mixture of formate, acetate and ethanol was produced.
growth_records.notes: Same chemostat setpoint (D=mu=0.135/h, td=5.13 h). No citrate in medium so no citrate uptake. Lactose uptake and product rates only in Fig 1/Fig 6 (graphical), left null. Lactose-limited pH 5.5 without citrate.

growth_records.medium: Chemically defined medium, lactose-limited, with citrate (Condition 3)

up.method: HPLC; biomass-specific rate reported graphically (Fig 1)
enzymatic (Roche kit); only 30% of citrate consumed at pH 7 lactose-limited (low citrate-lyase/OAA-decarboxylase activity at high pH), rate near the low end (~1 umol/min/gDW = 0.06 mmol/gDW/h)

sec.method: HPLC; ~29 mol% of end-products at pH 7 lactose-limited (mostly mixed-acid formate/acetate/ethanol)
HPLC (Fig 6); dominant mixed-acid product at pH 7
HPLC (Fig 6)

growth_records.cite: van Mastrigt, Oscar et al. Citrate, low pH and amino acid limitation induce citrate utilization in Lactococcus lactis biovar diacetylactis. Microbial Biotechnology (2018). DOI:10.1111/1751-7915.13031. [PMC5812246]
growth_records.method: reported (D=mu=0.135/h at steady state); td calculated ln2/0.135=5.13 h
growth_records.snippet: at pH 7, only 30% and 63% of the citrate were consumed in lactose and amino acid-limited cultures respectively ... Under lactose limitation, only 68% and 29% of the end-products were lactate at pH 5.5 and 7 respectively.
growth_records.notes: Chemostat D=mu=0.135/h, td=5.13 h. Only 30% citrate consumed at pH 7 lactose-limited; this condition has the lowest citrate utilization (paper: 'very low citrate utilization rate at pH 7 under lactose limitation'), corresponding to the ~1 umol/min/gDW low end of the 1-65 range (=0.06 mmol/gDW/h). Lactate 29 mol% of end-products. Numeric per-condition uptake/secretion rates only in figures.

growth_records.method: reported (D=mu=0.135/h at steady state); td calculated ln2/0.135=5.13 h
growth_records.snippet: Under lactose limitation, only 68% and 29% of the end-products were lactate at pH 5.5 and 7 respectively. Instead, a mixture of formate, acetate and ethanol was produced.
growth_records.notes: Chemostat D=mu=0.135/h, td=5.13 h. Lactose-limited pH 7 without citrate; mixed-acid fermentation (lactate 29 mol%). No citrate uptake (none supplied). Rates graphical only, left null.

growth_records.medium: Chemically defined medium, amino acid-limited, with citrate (Condition 5)

up.method: HPLC; biomass-specific rate reported graphically (Fig 1); incomplete consumption (residual >10 mM) under amino acid limitation
enzymatic (Roche kit); >99.7% consumed at pH 5.5; high biomass-specific citrate utilization at pH 5.5 + amino acid limitation (Fig 1)

sec.method: HPLC; >91 mol% of end-products under amino acid limitation (homolactic)
HPLC; produced at low pH in presence of citrate; highest acetoin yield on citrate at pH 5.5 (Fig 7)

growth_records.cite: van Mastrigt, Oscar et al. Citrate, low pH and amino acid limitation induce citrate utilization in Lactococcus lactis biovar diacetylactis. Microbial Biotechnology (2018). DOI:10.1111/1751-7915.13031. [PMC5812246]
growth_records.method: reported (D=mu=0.135/h at steady state); td calculated ln2/0.135=5.13 h
growth_records.snippet: Lactate was the major end-product (> 91 mol%) under amino acid limitation independent of the pH ... more than 99.7% was consumed at pH 5.5 ... the biomass-specific citrate utilization rates were higher at pH 5.5 and under amino acid limitat
growth_records.notes: Chemostat D=mu=0.135/h, td=5.13 h. Amino acid-limited pH 5.5 with citrate: homolactic (lactate >91 mol%), citrate consumption >99.7%, high biomass-specific citrate uptake. Citrate utilization capacity range across all conditions 1-65 umol/min/gDW (=0.06-3.9 mmol/gDW/h). Per-condition numeric rates graphical only (Fig 1).

growth_records.medium: Chemically defined medium, amino acid-limited, without citrate (Condition 6)

up.method: HPLC; biomass-specific rate reported graphically (Fig 1); incomplete consumption under amino acid limitation

sec.method: HPLC; >91 mol% of end-products under amino acid limitation (homolactic)

growth_records.cite: van Mastrigt, Oscar et al. Citrate, low pH and amino acid limitation induce citrate utilization in Lactococcus lactis biovar diacetylactis. Microbial Biotechnology (2018). DOI:10.1111/1751-7915.13031. [PMC5812246]
growth_records.method: reported (D=mu=0.135/h at steady state); td calculated ln2/0.135=5.13 h
growth_records.snippet: Lactate was the major end-product (> 91 mol%) under amino acid limitation independent of the pH. This metabolic profile is comparable with the profile found in batch cultures of L. lactis.
growth_records.notes: Chemostat D=mu=0.135/h, td=5.13 h. Amino acid-limited pH 5.5 without citrate: homolactic (lactate >91 mol%). No citrate supplied. Rates graphical only, left null.

growth_records.medium: Chemically defined medium, amino acid-limited, with citrate (Condition 7)

up.method: HPLC; biomass-specific rate reported graphically (Fig 1); incomplete consumption under amino acid limitation
enzymatic (Roche kit); 63% of citrate consumed at pH 7 amino acid-limited (Fig 1)

sec.method: HPLC; >91 mol% of end-products under amino acid limitation (homolactic)
HPLC; lower acetoin yield on citrate at pH 7 than at pH 5.5 (Fig 7)

growth_records.cite: van Mastrigt, Oscar et al. Citrate, low pH and amino acid limitation induce citrate utilization in Lactococcus lactis biovar diacetylactis. Microbial Biotechnology (2018). DOI:10.1111/1751-7915.13031. [PMC5812246]
growth_records.method: reported (D=mu=0.135/h at steady state); td calculated ln2/0.135=5.13 h
growth_records.snippet: at pH 7, only 30% and 63% of the citrate were consumed in lactose and amino acid-limited cultures respectively ... Lactate was the major end-product (> 91 mol%) under amino acid limitation independent of the pH.
growth_records.notes: Chemostat D=mu=0.135/h, td=5.13 h. Amino acid-limited pH 7 with citrate: 63% citrate consumed; homolactic (lactate >91 mol%). Per-condition numeric rates graphical only (Fig 1).

growth_records.medium: Chemically defined medium, amino acid-limited, without citrate (Condition 8)

up.method: HPLC; biomass-specific rate reported graphically (Fig 1); incomplete consumption under amino acid limitation

sec.method: HPLC; >91 mol% of end-products under amino acid limitation (homolactic)

growth_records.cite: van Mastrigt, Oscar et al. Citrate, low pH and amino acid limitation induce citrate utilization in Lactococcus lactis biovar diacetylactis. Microbial Biotechnology (2018). DOI:10.1111/1751-7915.13031. [PMC5812246]
growth_records.method: reported (D=mu=0.135/h at steady state); td calculated ln2/0.135=5.13 h
growth_records.snippet: Lactate was the major end-product (> 91 mol%) under amino acid limitation independent of the pH.
growth_records.notes: Chemostat D=mu=0.135/h, td=5.13 h. Amino acid-limited pH 7 without citrate: homolactic (lactate >91 mol%). No citrate supplied. Rates graphical only, left null. NOTE: overnight pre-culture (inoculum) grown in M17 + 0.5% lactose; M17 shown by enzymatic assay to contain 0.80 +/- 0.07 mM citrate (relevant caveat for citrate-induction studies, not a growth-rate datum).
